attachment.name: Attachment Styles
attachment.subtitle: How you manage closeness and distance in relationships
attachment.description: Based on the ECR-R model (Experiences in Close Relationships). Measures two dimensions: Anxiety (fear of abandonment) and Avoidance (discomfort with intimacy).

questions.text: I feel uncomfortable when someone gets emotionally close to me.
I prefer not to show my partner how I really feel.
When my partner wants a lot of intimacy, I feel tense.
I find it difficult to rely emotionally on my partner.
I prefer not to depend on the people I love.
I'm not comfortable sharing my deepest thoughts with others.
I'm afraid of becoming too attached to my partner.
I find it hard to let partners get emotionally close to me.
I avoid opening up even when I genuinely care about the person.
I feel uncomfortable when I'm asked for direct emotional support.
I'm afraid of being abandoned by my partner.
I need frequent confirmation that my partner loves me.
When my partner is unavailable, I feel anxious.
I often worry that my partner is interested in someone else.
I fear my partner doesn't care about me as much as I care about them.
I worry about not being interesting enough for my partner.
When I don't hear from my partner for a few hours, I feel uneasy.
The idea that my partner might leave me deeply distresses me.
I get nervous if my partner doesn't respond to messages quickly.
I often need to feel that my partner is satisfied with our relationship.

loveLanguages.name: Love Languages
loveLanguages.subtitle: How you prefer to give and receive affection
loveLanguages.description: Based on Gary Chapman's model. Identifies your primary love language among the 5 love languages.

questions.text: I feel loved when my partner explicitly tells me how much they care about me.
Sincere compliments recharge me emotionally more than any gesture.
A verbal insult hurts me more than an unkind action.
I feel loved when my partner dedicates time just to me, without phone or distractions.
The most important thing for me is that my partner is present, not what we do.
I feel neglected when my partner seems elsewhere even if physically here.
When my partner brings me even a small unexpected gift, it makes me feel special.
For me, gifts aren't objects — they're visible symbols of my partner's love.
I hold on to objects or symbols that my partner has given me.
I feel loved when my partner helps me with something practical without being asked.
A partner who does things for me speaks louder than a thousand words.
I feel unloved when my partner promises to do something and doesn't follow through.
A hug, held hand, or caress calms me and makes me feel connected.
Physical contact (even non-sexual) is essential for my well-being in a relationship.
Lack of physical touch in a relationship makes me feel distant and alone.

bigFive.name: OCEAN Profile
bigFive.subtitle: Your 5 dominant personality traits
bigFive.description: Short version of the Big Five model. Measures the 5 core traits: Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, Neuroticism.

questions.text: I'm curious and love exploring new and complex ideas.
I deeply appreciate art, music, or literature.
I often have original thoughts and a vivid imagination.
I'm open to viewpoints very different from my own.
I'm organized and methodical in how I approach things.
I always follow through on what I start, even when it gets hard.
I meet deadlines and keep commitments rigorously.
I'm detail-oriented and it bothers me to make mistakes.
In groups, I like being the center of attention.
I feel energized after socializing for a long time.
I'm naturally outgoing and talk easily with strangers.
I prefer being with others rather than alone.
I'm genuinely interested in the well-being of others.
In a conflict, I always seek a fair compromise.
I find it easy to forgive people who have hurt me.
It comes naturally to me to be considerate of others' needs.
I worry often, even about things others find trivial.
I get easily upset by stress or difficulties.
I tend to feel anxious or emotionally tense.
I sometimes feel overwhelmed by emotions.

communication.name: Communication Style
communication.subtitle: Rapport Talk vs Report Talk
communication.description: Based on Deborah Tannen's research. Identifies whether you tend to communicate to build connection (Rapport) or to exchange information and solve problems (Report).

questions.text: When a friend tells me about a problem, I share a similar experience to make them feel less alone.
In a conversation, I care more about understanding how the other feels than finding a solution.
When discussing something, the listening process feels as important as the outcome.
I often share personal things about myself to create intimacy and connection.
In discussions, I seek consensus and harmony more than winning the point.
When someone describes a problem, my instinct is to suggest practical solutions.
In conversations, I prefer to get straight to the point without detours.
I find it frustrating to talk about a problem for a long time without reaching a concrete conclusion.
In discussions, content and facts seem more important to me than emotional tone.
When someone talks to me, I focus on what they should do, not on how they feel.
